text:
Integrating Data Across Oscillatory Power Bands Predicts the Epileptogenic Zone: the Frequency Range Explorer Epileptogenic Zone (FREEZ) Identification Algorithm

Authors: Sean O'Leary 1, † , Anne-Cecile Lesage 1, † , Liliana Camarillo-Rodriguez 1 , Oliver Zhou 1 , Diosely Silveira 2 , Jiefei Wang 3 , Sameer A. Sheth 4 , Michael S. Beauchamp 5 , Zhengjia Wang 5 , John F. Magnotti 5 , Patrick J. Karas 1,6

Abstract

Epilepsy affects over 70 million people globally. One-third of people with focal epilepsy have drug-resistant  epilepsy.  Identification  and  removal  of  the  site  of  onset  of  seizures,  termed  the epileptogenic  zone  (EZ),  is  the  most  successful  treatment  to  stop  seizures  in  these  people. Implanting electrodes into the brain with intracranial electroencephalography (iEEG) is the gold standard diagnostic test for identifying the EZ. But identification of the EZ with iEEG remains challenging in many cases. We developed a novel computational methodology that integrates mean power across delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma, and high gamma frequencies over time to identify the EZ. A machine learning model was trained to predict electrodes within the EZ using publicly available data from 21 patients. In patients that were seizure free after surgery, electrodes within the EZ had significantly higher area under the curve (AUC) for mean power over time in the first 20 seconds after a seizure compared to electrodes outside the EZ in the alpha (p = 0.001), beta (p =  0.001),  gamma  (p  <0.0001),  and  high  gamma  (p  =  0.0003)  ranges.  Additionally,  electrodes within the EZ in patients that became seizure-free after surgery had significantly higher AUC compared to electrodes marked within the EZ in patients who did not become seizure-free after surgery in the gamma (p = 0.0006) and high gamma (p <0.0001) power ranges. Leave-one-out patient cross validation of the machine learning model yielded a 95.7% positive predictive value and 80.6% specificity for identifying electrodes within the epileptogenic zone, and 90.5% accuracy for predicting seizure outcome based on a planned resection.

We implemented this algorithm into the open-source software tool "Reproducible Analysis and Visualization  of  iEEG"  (RAVE)  to  enable  users  to  reproduce  our  results  and  implement  this methodology  with  new  datasets,  creating  a  software  module  we  title  FREEZ.  The  software facilitates quantification of the spectral power changes during seizures, including displaying timefrequency spectrograms and projecting results across patient-specific 3D brain maps. Users can also adjust parameters for visualizing multiple frequency ranges from various time regions around seizure onsets in a web-browser-based interface.

Patrick Karas 301 University Blvd, JSA 9.300 Galveston, TX 77555-0517 pjkaras@utmb.edu

Running Title:

Frequency Range Explorer Epileptogenic Zone (FREEZ) Identification Algorithm

Key Words: drug-resistant  epilepsy,  epileptogenic  zone  localization,  iEEG  power  analysis,  multi-taper, FREEZ, RAVE

Introduction

Drug resistant epilepsy (DRE) is estimated to affect approximately 33% of patients, defined as failure to achieve sustained seizure freedom following the use of two tolerated and appropriately chosen anti-epileptic drug (AED) regiments. 1,2 DRE is associated with increased mortality rate, with 40% experiencing sudden unexpected death during epilepsy. 3 Surgical treatments offer the potential of seizure freedom for DRE patients, with reported seizure freedom rates of 58% to 78% following resection of the epileptogenic zone (EZ). 4 Historically, the EZ has been characterized as the portion of the brain that is both required and adequate for attaining freedom from seizures after surgical removal. 5,6 In cases when non-invasive exams (electroencephalography EEG, ) are not conclusive the clinical standard-of-care for EZ localization is visual interpretation of seizures from implanted intracranial electroencephalography (iEEG) electrode recordings by an epileptologist 7 . Epileptiform discharges are visually identified from iEEG recordings and correlated with their anatomic location and the patient's clinical features to identify the EZ. Seizure outcomes (seizure freedom  versus  continuation  of  seizures)  after  surgery  are  suggestive  of  whether  the  EZ  was correctly identified and removed during surgery or not. Using surgical outcomes as validation, computational epileptogenic zone identification algorithms (CEZIAs) have been developed to help clinicians identify the EZ. Different CEZIAs analyze different subsets of features selected from iEEG recordings, including the timing and anatomic distribution of these signals, extracting a subset of 'anatomical-electro-clinical correlations' that are central to defining the EZ. CEZIAs range from basic signal processing on individual channels (univariate) to examining channel pairs (bivariate) and whole-network connectivity (multivariate) 8 .

Several univariate methods are based on the analysis of the spectral content of the IEEG signals at  seizure  onset.  They  rely  on    the  different  frequency  patterns  that  have  been  clinically observed 9,10 .  Some CEZIAs demonstrated the correlation between EZ localization and high power in  the  beta  frequency  range 11,12 .  Bartolomei et  al. 13 developed  the  epileptogenic  index  method which relies on assessing the timing of changes in the ratio of energy between fast (beta and gamma range) and slow activities (alpha and theta range). In Grinenko et al. 14 , correlation between  the association of multi-band fast activity and low frequency suppression with EZ localization was reported. In Izumi et  al. 15 ,  the  relationship  between cooccurrence of  ictal direct current shifts (icDCs) and ictal high frequency oscillations and EZ localization was investigated. These studies demonstrate a complex interplay of neural activity across different frequency bands during seizure onset.  However,  a  unified  method  to  consider  all  frequency  patterns  correlating  with  EZ localization is missing.

Among spectrogram analysis methods, multitaper spectrogram analysis (MSA) provides accurate frequency  resolution 16 .  However,  despite  the  existence  of  publicly  available  libraries  for Spectrogram Analysis (SA) including the multitaper_toolbox 17 , there is no integration with iEEG localization  and  signal  processing  tools.  Also,  there  is  no  integration  that  is  paired  with visualization functionalities to explore and analyze the correlation of EZ localization and increased relative mean power: spatiotemporal visualization of the mean relative power by electrodes and brain mapping of multitaper spectrogram analysis (MSA) results.

To design a tool for aiding the EZ localization process through a unified MSA, we developed FREEZ (the Frequency Range Explorer Epileptogenic Zone) a web-based visualization tool to quantify and predict electrode classification and seizure outcomes based on changes in the spectral power around the seizure onset zone. FREEZ also allows for multi-patient analysis through scriptbased pipelining to generate both results as well as provide statistical analysis and prediction using ensemble  machine  learning 18 stacking  models  built  on  several  frequency  range  analysis.  The advantage of FREEZ is that it is opensource, does not rely on expensive commercial licenses and is integrated into RAVE R eproducible A nalysis and V isualization of intracranial E EG 19 , an NIH platform.  In  this  paper,  we  validate  the  performance  of  our  tool  to  assist  EZ  localization  by analyzing mean relative power on the publicly available Fragility multi center dataset 11 .

Material and Methods

Patient Data and Selection

The Fragility multi center dataset 11 , available publicly on the OpenNeuro platform, encompasses data from 35 patients distributed across four centers (NIH, JHH, UMCC and UMF). The iEEG data is structured in the BIDS format.  Electrode names and classification by epileptologists in the seizure onset zone are available. However, electrode localization is not available. The iEEG signal data is in the BrainVision format. A file features event markers annotated by clinicians that indicate the timing  of clinical events and  the onset/offset for each  seizure, both  clinically  and electrographically. These seizures are marked according to standard procedures within the clinical epilepsy workflow. In in this paper study, patients met the following criteria: (1) they underwent resection or laser ablation surgery guided by intracranial EEG using subdural grids, strips, and depth electrodes (ECoG); (2) they had at least one seizure recorded during intracranial monitoring, allowing for confirmatory testing of selected channels; (3) their recordings were at frequencies of 500 Hz or higher, suitable for high-range frequency analysis; and (4) detailed surgical outcomes, including Engel scores, were available. After exclusions, 21 patients remained in the study for a quantitative analysis focused on the relationship between MSA and the EZ. A total of 21 patients were included. The mean age at epilepsy onset was 19.5 ± 12 years (range: 13-59), and the mean age at the time of surgery was 36.39 ± 12.13 years (range: 13-59). The mean epilepsy duration before  surgery  was  16.89  years  (range:  4-41).  The  cohort  included  9  males,  9  females,  and  3 patients with unspecified sex. All patients underwent ECoG, with an average of 3.05 ± 0.84 ictal events recorded per patient (range: 1-4). Surgical targets were predominantly in the temporal lobe (N = 17), followed by the frontal lobe (N = 2) and the parietal lobe (N = 2). Post-surgery, 14 patients achieved seizure freedom (Engel I), while 7 did not. Among the non-seizure-free patients, 3 were classified as Engel II, 1 as Engel III, and 3 as Engel IV [ see Supplementary Table 1 ]. As in  the  study  by  Li et  al. 11 ,  we  assume  for the validation of our EZ localization tools that the annotated SOZ electrodes are inside the resected area for all patients and that they are a good estimate of the EZ in case of seizure freedom outcome.

iEEG data preprocessing in RAVE and YAEL

Raw signal has been preprocessed in RAVE 2 19 .  Data was notch filtered and common average referenced. These steps can be either be applied using the RAVE 2 GUI (graphical user interphase) or automatized with RAVE 2 pipelines which allow for script-based analysis without GUI use. Electrode  localization  can  be  either  preprocessed  with  YAEL 20 (patient  specific)  or  from standardized prelocalized format (MNI) on a template 3D brain.

Multitaper spectral analysis

To carry out MSA, we implemented the computation of spatiotemporal heatmap of electrode mean power for user-defined frequency range in R. The MSA is based on the multitaper R library 17 which  provide  tools  to  compute  the  multitaper  Fourier  transform 21 in  an  efficient  parallel implementation. To generate MSA results, the basic parameters include the step size or window length for analysis with respective default values 2.5s and 0.5s. The main lobe's bandwidth, in turn, defines the frequency resolution or the smallest detectable frequency difference, and its default value  is  set  to  3  for  optimal  resolution.    Additionally,  the  time-half  bandwidth  product  is determined by multiplying the window's length (N seconds) by half of the main lobe's bandwidth (BW/2).  These  values ensure accuracy for a wide  range of frequencies 11,17 .  Advanced customization  choices  include  setting  the  number  of  DPSS  tapers  used  for  spectrogram computation, specifying the minimum allowable NFFT size for manual zero-padding adjustment to  optimize  the  Fast  Fourier  Transform  (FFT)  computation,  selecting  taper  weights  (options include "unity," "eigen" for taper contribution weighted by eigenvalues, or "adaptive" to minimize broadband leakage), choosing a detrend method ("linear" for fitting a linear model and subtraction, "constant" for zero-mean data, or "off" for no detrending), and configuring the number of parallel CPU cores for processing.

Statistical  analysis  of  correlation  between  spectral  features  and  EZ localization

We have implemented multi-patient pipelining in RAVE 19 to allow for generation of MSA mean power heatmap results in .csv format for statistical analysis of correlation with EZ classifications. We tested this pipelining on the Fragility public data set, generating heatmaps 0s from seizure onset to 20s after seizure onset, with mean subtract baselining for all analyzed seizure to a time window -30s to -20s before seizure onset. We computed multitaper heatmap across six frequency bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma, and high gamma).

The resultant heatmaps were further statistically analyzed for each patient through both an average power over time plot as well as a statistic over time plot of the 10 th through 100 th power percentiles (in steps of 10) for two groups of electrodes: EZ and non-EZ-labeled electrodes.

The mean and standard deviation were calculated for the area under the curve (AUC) of the average power over time plot for EZ and non-EZ labeled electrodes stratified by post-surgical seizure freedom outcome. To evaluate significant differences in metrics between electrodes classified as within or outside the epileptogenic zone (EZ) for both seizure-free and non-seizure-free patients, pairwise Wilcoxon Rank Sum tests were conducted. Additionally, we compared metrics between seizure-free and non-seizure-free conditions across EZ and non-EZ channel average power AUCs. A Bonferroni correction was applied to all p-values to adjust for multiple testing, enhancing the reliability of the statistical assessments for multiple pairwise testing.

Ensemble  machine  learning  method  to  combine  multi-band  frequency analysis for electrode classification and patient outcome prediction

We employed a trained stacked random forest ensemble (SRFE) machine learning approach to predict  the  epileptogenic  zone  (EZ)  on  a  selected  patient  seizure  condition.  Specifically,  it necessitates a 20-second temporal window post-seizure onset for analysis, incorporating a series of  random  forest  algorithms,  which  have  been  trained  on  the  seizure-free  cohort  from  the Fragility 11 dataset. The algorithm processes individual predictions across six distinct frequency bands: delta (0.5-4 Hz), theta (4-8 Hz), alpha (8-13 Hz), beta (13-30 Hz), gamma (30-90 Hz), and high gamma (90-256 Hz), and subsequently aggregates these predictions to inform a final SRFE model that synthesizes the comprehensive spectral data to produce the final EZ predictions. We tested this model with first a large random forest model set to generate 100 decision trees for timefrequency features, with an additional feature weighting each frequency band by summing the power  of  all  time  windows  per  band.  This  was  compared  with  the  results  from  training  6 independent random forest models with 100 decision trees on each frequency band and averaging the outcome, as well as against an SRFE model using the H2O R package to make final predictions from the output probabilities. Thresholds for the prediction were analyzed, and the most specific model was selected for final analysis. The final model was then used to calculate the algorithm's performance metrics: AUC, accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV). For all cross validation, leave-one-out patient cross validation was employed, with majority voting (≥ 50%) across different seizure conditions for each patient to  select  final  predictions.  In  our  analysis,  the  importance  of  each  feature  was  determined  by extracting  the  feature  coefficient  from  the  SRFE  model.  A  larger  coefficient  for  base  models indicates a more significant impact on the SRFE model in enhancing prediction accuracy, thereby helping to prioritize which of the six frequency band features are most influential in predicting EZ classification. We then trained the final SRFE model on all seizure free patients, and subsequently tested  on  non-seizure  free  patients  to  confirm  selection  of  EZ  electrodes  corresponded  to  the ground truth of seizure freedom when removed.

Patient outcomes were predicted using time-series plot analysis. In this process, power percentiles of the EZ channels, ranging from the 10th to the 100th percentile in 10-percent increments, were normalized on a 0 to 1 scale. These normalized values were then divided by their corresponding percentiles from the non-EZ channels, also scaled between 1 and 10, resulting in a set of ratios. These ratios,  representing  relative  power  differences  for  each  percentile  across  all  plots,  were tailored for each patient, condition, and frequency band. Initially, these ratios were used to train a random forest  model  consisting  of  100  decision  trees  for  each  frequency  band.  Subsequently, correlations between the predictions across frequency bands were calculated using the Pearson correlation coefficient. Following this, an SRFE (Stacked Random Forest Ensemble) model was constructed using the H2O R package, incorporating stacked random forests from each of the six frequency ranges. The cutoff threshold for predictions was established based on the maximum model  accuracy.  For  all  cross-validation  procedures,  a  leave-one-out  patient  cross-validation method was used, along with majority voting (≥ 50%) across different seizure conditions for each patient to finalize predictions.

FREEZ  module:  a  toolbox  for  visualization  of  correlation  analysis between spectral features and EZ localization

The different processes described in the previous subsections have been integrated and are freely available through the FREEZ module GUI (Frequency Range Explorer Epileptogenic Zone) for visualization after processing and reproducibility of this study results: frequency range MSA mean power heatmap, statistical analysis of correlation between spectral features and EZ localization for individual  patients  through  electrode  grouping  and  prediction  results  of  SFRE  for  electrode localization  of  an  individual  patient.  FREEZ  is  integrated  as  a  RAVE  module  written  in  R programming  language,  HTML  (hypertext  markup  language),  CSS  (cascading  style  sheets), JavaScript, and shell. 22 RAVE integration allows users to run on laptops, desktops, or in the cloud, with seamless integration between platforms as all module interaction is performed through a web browser. 22 Because all computing in RAVE can be performed on any server or user machine, interaction with the module does not require independent computation. 22 IEEG data set have to be preprocessed  in  RAVE  2  as  described  in  the    second  subsection  of  this  section  for  further processing in FREEZ.

The advanced parameters of an MSA to compute mean power in a user-defined frequency range can be toggled in the FREEZ GUI ( Supplemental Figure 1 A and B) . Following processing of the multi-taper results, the user can choose the epoch time window around the seizure offset. The user can  choose  between  several  conditions  of  the  RAVE  preprocessed  signal  e.g.  ictal  signal  of different recorded seizures, and toggle between conditions for analysis. The user can also mean subtract baseline the heatmaps to a selected baseline condition, with the option the duration from the start of the baseline condition that the user wishes to utilize, where X = X - mean ( X B ), where again X represents the original heatmap and X B the selected baseline condition for the selected baseline duration. The options for this are presented in Supplemental Figure 1C . Frequency ranges and time axis can be modified for the analysis condition, with the analysis options allowing for sliding bars to be utilized for tailoring the analysis results. We also provide buttons for preset frequency ranges of interest for user convenience encompassing Delta [0.5-4 Hz], Theta [4-8 Hz], Alpha [8-13 Hz], Beta [13-30 Hz], Gamma [30-90 Hz] and High Gamma [90+ Hz] frequency ranges. The user can also define additional analysis frequency ranges.

Plotting options are also given with the ability for the user to choose between a non-normalized heatmap view, or a min-max normalization of the heatmap by time window. For the min-max normalization of the heatmap by time window, a mean power value per time window is computed and max-normalized by time window in the range [-1:1], e.g. the electrode with value one is the one with highest power in the time window: X norm ( i , j ) = X ( i , j ) - min( X Wt ) / max( X Wt ) - min( X Wt ) where X represents the original heatmap, X i,j is the element at row i and column j of X , W t represents the time window t , X Wt represents the submatrix of X within time window W t , Max( W t ) represents the maximum value within the time window W t ,  and X norm represents the final normalized data matrix. Color maps can be selected to change all the plotting value scales.

The user can also input electrodes deemed SOZ or Resect for plotting, with SOZ coded blue, Resect Purple, and contacts with overlap between the input Resect and SOZ colored Green on the y axis. The resultant power-over-time per electrode plot can also be grouped by provided Resect or SOZ contacts to aid in visualization through the grouping of channels of interest, as well as have the electrode labels toggled between numeric labels. The user can also convert the power units (P) supplied by the multitaper result into decibels (dB), where dB=10 x log 10 (P). These options are all provided in Supplemental Figure 1E .  The power over time plot as seen in Figure 1D can  be correlated with the raw signal result in Figure 1C for visualization of the spectral features extracted from signal data by the multitaper. Electrodes can be selected by their number and be colored by labels, here in blue for the electrodes annotated as SOZ by epileptologists. Users can see either their number from the channel files in the BIDS format or their name as in Figure 1C. Heatmap visualization can be toggled to display electrodes grouped as resected on top and the remaining electrodes on the bottom. This can be useful to check visually that marked electrodes correlate with high relative power.

The module GUI provides also visualization of the statistical analysis of the MSA heatmap results by electrodes grouping (see Figure 1E and 1F). The power over time plot as seen in Figure 1D can be correlated with the raw signal result in Figure 1C for visualization of the spectral features extracted from signal data by the multitaper. FREEZ GUI offer the visualization of the average power over time plot for PT01 with specified EZ channels. The statistic over time plot can also be stratified,  with  stacked  heatmaps  for  other,  resect,  and/or  soz  channels  divided  by  a  red  line.

Moreover, the user can visualize the quantile statistics over time plot which provides additional analysis of differences in the power between selected electrodes [ Figure 1F ].

FREEZ offers a similar brain visualization tool than YAEL 18 . It uses open-source R code to process the workflow and the shiny package for the user interface. FREEZ provides the ability to visualize electrodes on a template, or patient specific brain model. Additionally, we provide an electrode projection functionality to take in account brain shape variability (see Figure 1A and 1B ). FREEZ allows patient specific visualization of brain cortex surface mesh in the FreeSurfer format 23 . This data  can  be  computed  using  the  FreeSurfer  software  and  patient  specific  T1  MRI  (magnetic resonance imaging) imaging. With FREEZ brain mapping functionality, the MSA heatmap can be projected over the patient specific or template 3D brain for visualization of anatomical position, as well as location of selected resect and soz channels, and temporal analysis through a sliding bar of changes in heatmap values over time allowing for viewing of the dynamic evolution of the heatmap, color coded based on the color template selected by the user [ Figure 1B ].

Finally, FREEZ allows to pipeline the whole workflow for (see Figure 2 ) through integration to the RAVE 2 framework for multi-patient analysis.

Data Availability

The source repository is accessible on https://github.com/seanmnoleary/ravepipelines/tree/sean_update/modules/multitaper_explorer with installation guidelines for the Windows,  MacOS  and  Linux  platforms  on  https://openwetware.org/wiki/Karas_Lab.  The  web page  https://openwetware.org/wiki/RAVE  provides  documentation  to  use  the  software  and integrate  it  for  signal  preprocessing  and  electrode  localization  with  two  open-source  software: RAVE 222 and YAEL24. Users can reach out for help about installation and software use in a dedicated Slack support channel

Results

FREEZ  allows  multi-component  visual  and  statistic  assessment  of correlation  between  MSA  results  and  EZ  localization  for  individual patients

Figure 1 demonstrates how the visualization functionalities of FREEZ can be used for a multi component analysis of the correlation between MSA results and EZ localization for an individual patient, here patient PT01. Figure 1C and 1D demonstrate how the MSA heatmap results correlates with spectral patterns in the raw signal. Here solely the electrodes marked in blue as in the EZ display high power in the gamma frequency range, thus the heatmap can be used to predict visually this patient seizure outcome as a true positive. Figures 1E and 1F allow another confirmation of this true positive prediction thanks to statistical analysis of two groups EZ and non-EZ labelled electrodes.

For six patients out of the twenty-one patients used in this study, we were able to retrieve patientspecific MRI imaging, patient specific cortex and electrode localization from the patient data set used in Diamond et al. 25 ( PT01, PT2, PT3, PT6, PT14, PT15). 3D brain mapping in FREEZ for patient PT01 confirms the correlation between high power in the gamma range and EZ localization (see Figure 1A and 1B).

MSA  multi-patient  analysis  using  FREEZ  pipelines  reveals  that  high power in alpha, beta, gamma and high gamma range correlates with EZ localization

We used the pipelining functionalities of FREEZ on the Fragility Data Set 11 to generate heatmaps in six frequency ranges for 21 patients with a total of x seizure conditions. Electrodes labeled as resected EZ had a significantly higher area under the curve (AUC) for mean power over time in the first 20 seconds following seizure onset compared to non-EZ electrodes in seizure-free patients. This was observed in the alpha (p = 0.001), beta (p = 0.001), gamma (p <0.0001), and high gamma (p  =  0.0003)  frequency  ranges.  Additionally,  EZ  electrodes  in  seizure-free  patients  had  a significantly higher AUC than EZ electrodes in non-seizure-free patients for average power over time in the gamma (p = 0.0006) and high gamma (p <0.001) frequency ranges [ Figure 3 ]. These results highlight that significant spectral information for EZ localization is contained in several frequency ranges MSA. Thus, it justifies combining them through a SFRE model to design an electrode classification tool.

SFRE  model  performs  significantly  better  than  RF  models  based  on individual  frequency  ranges  for  electrode  classification  and  patient outcome prediction

We used the multi-patient multi-frequency data generated previously for multipatient statistical analysis to run computational experiments on Random Forest model performance. The optimal threshold  for  random  forest  output  probabilities  was  90.67%  [ Figure  4B ].  The  prediction  of electrodes within the EZ by the SRFE model resulted in 22 true positive predictions within the EZ and one false positive [ Figure 4A ]. True positives were identified in 8 out of the 14 seizure-free patients:  PT01,  PT02, PT08, PT13, PT15, PT17, UMMC002, and JH105. Feature importance, measured by min-max scaled mean squared error decrease, was highest in the beta range (0.66 ± 0.07), followed by high gamma (0.57 ± 0.08), delta (0.51 ± 0.06), alpha (0.43 ± 0.07), gamma (0.39 ± 0.05), and theta (0.18 ± 0.07). The SRFE model, trained on seizure-free patients and tested on non-seizure-free patients, identified 2 EZ electrodes that were not removed and 3 EZ electrodes that were removed [ Figure 4D ]. The SFRE performs significantly better on electrode classification than an RF model based on individual frequency ranges (see supplementary Figure 2).

After computing an SFRE model based on multi-patient and multi-frequency range analysis, one can visualize electrode classification results on individual patients. Figure 5 illustrate visualization of SFRE model results for four prediction patient cases. It shows the heatmap results in the gamma range for a [0:20] time epoch around seizure onset. The heatmap displays the electrode numbers and the time in seconds, respectively on the y and x axes. Electrodes marked as EZ are highlighted in  blue  and  stacked  on  the  heatmap  bottom.  Cross  marks  highlight  electrodes  that  have  been classified as EZ by the SFRE model from the multi-patient analysis. We can see on Figure 5B another visualization of electrode classification result on PT15, a seizure free patient, with high gamma power outside of the resected EZ. As such, our SRFE model selected electrodes both within and outside of resected tissue, resulting in the one false positive result. PT10 demonstrates the predictive  power  of  the  SRFE  model  on  a  non-seizure  free  patient,  with  the  expected  EZ determined by the SRFE model to be outside of the tissue that was resected. This can be seen by the EZ electrodes on the heatmap bottom that do not correlate well with high relative power in the area  predicted  by  the  SRFE  model  in Figure  5C. Finally,  PT06  represents  a  patient  where potentially  sparse  sampling  resulted  in  relative  spectral  power  not  being  predictive  of  which electrodes  should  be  removed.  High  relative  power  is  within  the  removed  electrodes,  and  this correlates the SRFE model predictions as seen in Figure 5D , however the patient was non-seizure free postoperatively.

Finally,  patient  outcomes  were  predicted  using  random  forests,  each  constrained  to  one  of  six frequency ranges: delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma, and high gamma. The highest Area Under the Curve (AUC) was observed for the delta band at 0.77, followed by beta at 0.74, gamma at 0.67, high  gamma  at  0.57,  theta  at  0.52,  and  alpha  at  0.51  [ Figure  6A ].  Analysis  of  the  Pearson correlation  coefficients  between  frequency  band  predictions  revealed  the  strongest  correlation between high gamma and gamma (R² = 0.83), and between beta and alpha (R² = 0.39). On the other hand, negative correlation was seen between alpha and high gamma (R² = -0.26) and alpha and gamma (R² = -0.13)  [ Figure 6C ]. Leave-one-out cross validation, using a threshold of 71.22% likelihood  from  the  probability  output  of  the  SRFE  model,  yielded  13  true  positives,  6  true negatives, 1 false positive, and 1 false negative [ Figure 6B ]. Overall, the SRFE model achieved an  accuracy  of  90.48%,  with  a  PPV  of  92.86%,  NPV  of  85.71%,  sensitivity  of  92.86%,  and specificity of 85.71%. Statistics over time plots used for model training when predicting seizure outcome can be seen in Figure 6D ,  demonstrating both a seizure free patient (PT08) and nonseizure free patient (PT10), both with correctly predicted outcomes by the SRFE model.

Discussion

Our MSA statistical findings on this multi-patient study indicate that specific frequency ranges, particularly  suppression  in  the  lower  frequencies  including  delta  and  theta,  and  high  power  in gamma and high gamma, play critical roles in identifying the EZ. These results agree with previous studies computing metrics of this spectral pattern as the Epileptogenic index 13 and the Fingerprint method 14 .  The distribution of frequency band feature importance for the SFRE model indicates that the beta, delta and high gamma frequency range are significant predictor which is confirmed by several studies 11,15,25 . Our study confirms complex interplay between spectral features in the EZ.  Thus,  it  is  important  to  consider  all  frequency  bands  features  for  EZ  classification generalization on diverse patient data set.

Our multi-patient results suggests that SFRE using MSA of IEEG signals in six different frequency ranges (delta, alpha, theta, beta, gamma and high gamma) is a relevant tool to provide a unified exploration  of  the  correlation  between  spectral  features  and  EZ  localization  to  automatically classify  electrodes.  This  SFRE  performance  results  encourage  further  experiments  including results  based  on  other  features  than  spectral  features  like  network  analysis,  phase  locked  high gamma and source localization.

The FREEZ software developed for this study provides a user-friendly, web-based tool designed to visualize and detect changes in spectral power associated with the onset of a seizure. The tool is  tailored  to  be  accessible  to  both  researchers  and  clinicians,  offering  a  unified  platform  for informed decision-making during SOZ localization. Developed as a module in R and seamlessly integrated into RAVE,  an  NIH-supported  platform, this tool displays a time-frequency spectrogram and a template brain with a drop-down menu. The 3D brain map allows users to view both the location of electrodes within the brain and those that exhibit biomarker properties.

This tool encompasses a range of valuable features to enhance the user experience. Firstly, it offers a user-friendly interface for both individual patient and multi-patient analysis. Our web-based tool prioritizes  usability,  ensuring  that  users,  even  without  programming  backgrounds,  can  easily navigate and utilize its functionalities. Secondly, the tool is seamlessly integrated into the RAVE platform. Our module streamlines the user experience, eliminating the need to switch between multiple platforms for preprocessing, analysis, and result visualization. Further, users can access and implement other RAVE features. Additionally, recognizing the variability inherent in epilepsy data, we provide users with the flexibility to modify settings and parameters as necessary. This adaptability enhances the tool versatility across diverse epilepsy datasets. Lastly, the module goes beyond  calculating  and  displaying  results,  but  enables  real-time  dynamic  adjustments  to parameters, facilitating exploration of data. This feature fosters a more dynamic and personalized analysis experience for users, allowing them to tailor their analysis in real-time.

Furthermore,  the  open-source  nature  of  our  software  aims  to  democratize  access  to  advanced epileptic seizure analysis tools and result reproducibility, potentially bridging the gap in epilepsy surgery referrals and facilitating more informed clinical decisions. Through a user-friendly, webbased interface, our tool not only simplifies the analysis process for researchers and clinicians alike but also provides a flexible and dynamic platform for the exploration of epileptic seizure data, enhancing the potential for personalized

Within the current framework of managing patients with DRE, financial impediments are evident, as access to epilepsy surgery is constrained due to the necessity for referrals to a restricted pool of highly specialized physicians. 26-28 By making our open-source software freely available, we hope to address this challenge by allowing for it to be leveraged as a quantitative tool to assist with surgical decision making.

Limitations of this study

For fragility 29 patients the SOZ contacts are an estimation of the resect contacts and the ground truth EZ. Post-surgical T1 MRI was not available. Because XYZ locations were available for only six patients in the fragility 29 multicenter patients, the exploration of  the 3D brain plot functionality for correlation between EZ and MSA heatmap results.  We are currently collecting retrospective iEEG data at our institution with patient specific imaging allowing accurate electrode localization (preoperative T1 MRI, CT post implantation) and accurate mapping of the resected area. This data will allow exploration of 3D brain mapping for EZ localization.

The results of this study are limited to twenty-one patients. We plan in the future to test our module on larger patient number thanks to our retrospective patient study. However, the tool is freely available to researchers to perform similar study on independent data set than the one used in this paper. Thus, we encourage further testing of our tool.

Figure/Table Legends:

Figure 1: FREEZ module results on PT01. 3D brain projection showing marked SOZ channels (1A), as well as the projected multitaper result over time (1B) in the beta frequency range [13 - 30 Hz]. Plots are shown generated from the module with the raw signal over time (1C), multitaper power result over time per electrode (1D), average power over time line plot (1E), and time plot of the 10 th through 100 th power percentiles (in steps of 10) for EZ and non-EZ channels (1F), with data from -5 to 10s around seizure onset, which is marked by a green dotted line Figure 2: FREEZ module workflow. Data and user defined inputs shown in blue, intermediary processes in green, module visualization options in pink, and outputs in orange.

Figure 3: Area under the curve (AUC) for mean power over time from 0 to 20s following seizure onset. Seizure free and non-seizure free patients separated by marked EZ and non EZ channels in the delta (3A), theta (3B), alpha (3C), beta (3D), gamma (3E), and high gamma (3F) ranges. Significant (P < 0.002 following Bonferroni correction) pairwise comparisons marked with *.

Figure 4: Machine learning results from the stacked random forest ensemble (SRFE). (4A) Results of leave-one-out patient cross-validation. (4B) Positive predictive value (PPV) vs. true positives (TP) for changing threshold values (shown in blue text), with maximum true positives with PPV ≥ 95% marked by horizontal and vertical dotted red lines, annotated with threshold, TP, false positives (FP), and PPV. (4C) Distribution of feature importance for the SRFE showing mean squared  error  (MSE)  when  each  base  model  representing  a  frequency  band  was  sequentially excluded  and  the  SRFE  retrained.  (4D)  Results  of  EZ  predictions  applied  to  non-seizure  free patients  showing  distribution  between  resected  and  non-resected  tissue,  juxtaposed  against seizure-free patients.

Figure 5: Patient multitaper heatmap results in the beta (13 - 30 Hz) range. EZ channels (marked blue) are organized at the bottom of the heatmap and delineated with a blue box. Predicted EZ channels by the random forest are highlighted with an asterisk (*). (5A) PT08, who was seizurefree, had all predicted EZ resected. (5B) PT15, also seizure-free, had one predicted EZ removed, and one not removed. (5C) PT10, who was not seizure-free, had predicted EZ not removed. (5D) PT06, also not seizure-free, had the predicted EZ removed.

Figure 6: Results from patient outcome predictions. 6A displays the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for individual frequency bands calculated using random forests, as well as the AUC for the final  stacked  random  forest  ensemble  (SRFE)  along  with  accuracy,  positive  predictive  value (PPV), and negative predictive value at the threshold cutoff with maximum accuracy. 6B presents the results from the leave-one-out patient cross validation at the maximum accuracy cutoff, which includes true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives from the SRFE model. 6C illustrates the Pearson correlation coefficients between output probabilities. Finally, 6D shows the statistics over time plots for a seizure free and non-seizure free patient in the beta frequency range [13 - 30 Hz].

Supplementary Table 1: Characteristics of the included patients. Details include demographics, neural recording, surgical procedure, and outcome

Supplementary Table 2: Area under the curve (AUC) average power for included patients in a time window from seizure onset to 20s following seizure onset. Data is mean baselined to a time window -30s to -20s before seizure onset. P < 0.002 is considered significant following Bonferroni correction for multiple P-Value testing. * indicates significance.

Supplementary Figure 1: Selection panels provided as user interface options in the FREEZ module. Basic parameter selection options include frequency range, window size, step size, and time-half  bandwidth  product  (1A),  while  advanced  options  cover  the  number  of  DPSS  tapers, minimum allowable NFFT size, taper weights, detrend options, and parallel computing options (1B). Analysis options allow for selection of baselining condition and duration (1C), and analysis frequency and time ranges, with default buttons for predefined ranges in delta (0.5-4 Hz), theta (48 Hz), alpha (8-13 Hz), beta (13-30 Hz), gamma (30-90 Hz), and high gamma (90+ Hz) (1D). Plotting  options  include  selection  of  normalization  method,  color  map,  decibel  units  toggle, definition of seizure onset zone and resect electrodes (colored blue and purple respectively, with overlap in green),  grouping  of  SOZ/Resect  channels  at  the  bottom  of  the  map,  and  toggle  for electrode labels (1E). Machine learning options include prediction capabilities and visualization on heatmaps (1F).

Supplementary Figure 2: Machine learning results on electrode selection for multiple models tested machine learning models. Positive predictive value (PPV) (y-axis) vs. true positives (x-axis) for three tested machine learning models on time-frequency features across six frequency ranges (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma, high gamma). (2A) Simple random forest model, (2B) frequency-specific random forests with stacked predictions, and (2C) stacked random forest ensemble (SRFE). Blue numbers represent applied thresholds to the output model probabilities. Dotted red lines mark the greatest number of true positives achieved with a PPV ≥ 95% considered the optimal threshold, with intersecting horizontal and vertical dotted red lines specifying the optimal threshold, as well as the number of true positives (TP), false positives (FP), and PPV at that optimal threshold.

metadata.preprint_title: Integrating Data Across Oscillatory Power Bands Predicts the Epileptogenic Zone: the Frequency Range Explorer Epileptogenic Zone (FREEZ) Identification Algorithm

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Integrating Data Across Oscillatory Power Bands Predicts the Epileptogenic Zone: the Frequency Range Explorer Epileptogenic Zone (FREEZ) Identification Algorithm
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: O'Leary, S.; Lesage, A.-C.; Camarillo-Rodriguez, L.; Zhou, O.; Silveira, D.; Wang, J.; Sheth, S.; Beauchamp, M. S.; Wang, Z.; Magnotti, J. F.; Karas, P. J.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Accurate identification of the seizure onset zone (SOZ) using intracranial electroencephalography (iEEG) remains challenging. Although diverse methods have leveraged spectral features to classify patient outcomes, few approaches focus on identifying individual electrodes within the SOZ or integrate a broad spectrum of frequency ranges within a single model. We developed an interpretable machine learning model that integrates power across delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma, and high-gamma frequencies over time to identify the SOZ. For 1,511 electrodes implanted across 21 patients, we computed the mean spectral power in each frequency band for the first 20 seconds after seizure onset and analyzed the differences in power between SOZ and non-SOZ electrodes. In patients who were seizure-free after surgery (n = 14), electrodes within the SOZ showed significantly higher area under the curve (AUC) for mean power over time in the first 20 seconds after seizure onset compared to electrodes outside the SOZ in the alpha (p = 0.0272), beta (p = 0.0263), gamma (p = 0.0013), and high gamma (p = 0.0086) ranges. Additionally, electrodes within the SOZ in patients that became seizure-free after surgery had significantly higher AUC compared to equivalent electrodes in patients who did not become seizure-free after surgery (n = 7) in the gamma (p = 0.0145) and high gamma (p = 0.0024) power ranges. We trained a stacked random forest ensemble model using these features over time to label electrodes within the SOZ. Leave-one-out patient cross validation of the machine learning model yielded a 96.6% positive predictive value and 99.9% specificity for identifying electrodes within the SOZ. Our dataset included a diverse array of seizure onset patterns, which were all classified accurately by the model. A second model was trained to predict post-operative seizure freedom, yielding 95.2% accuracy for predicting seizure outcome based on a planned resection. This two-model design mirrors clinical workflow, first localizing SOZ electrodes to support surgical planning, then predicting outcome based on a surgical plan. An advantage of our interpretable machine learning approach is the ability to interrogate our models to understand how predictions are made. For electrode classification, the model weighed beta (0.66 {+/-} 0.07), high gamma (0.54 {+/-} 0.06), and delta (0.51 {+/-} 0.06) power bands most heavily. Viewing the models frequency band weights over time reveals that the model identified a pattern resembling the "fingerprint of the epileptogenic zone", reinforcing the importance of this dominant fundamental neurophysiologic signature of seizure onset.
